1: chokmah banta beitah — “Wisdom has built her house.” The shift to perfective aspect (baneta, perfect) grounds the narrative: Wisdom’s house already exists, complete, ready to receive guests. Chatzevah 'ammudeyha shiv'ah — “she has hewn her seven pillars.” Chatzav (H2672, to hew, cut stone) suggests deliberate architectural labor; the seven pillars ('amudim, H5982) are the structural foundation of the building. Seven in Hebrew numerology signifies completeness. The Wisdom-house is the designed-and-built environment for the wisdom-life; its seven pillars have been interpreted as the seven books of the Torah, the seven days of creation, or simply completeness of preparation.
2: tavchah tivchah — “she has slaughtered her slaughter.” The intensive form (tavchah tivchah, the cognate object construction) emphasizes the abundance and completeness of the preparation: the feast is not incidental but central. Maskhah yeinah — “she has mixed her wine”: wine mixed with spices or water was the standard form for a feast. The parallel between Wisdom’s feast (slaughtered meat, mixed wine, prepared table) and the shelamim-feast of the adulteress in 7:14 is deliberately inverted: both offer a banquet, but wisdom’s leads to life and the seductress’s leads to Sheol.
3: shalachah na'aroteyha — “she has sent out her servant girls.” The plural na'arot (servant girls, attendants) makes wisdom’s household a substantial establishment. The servants call from the gappei meromei qaret — the “heights of the city” (literally ‘the wings/highest points of the city’). The elevated calling-point ensures maximum public visibility and reach — wisdom’s invitation is issued from the most prominent position available.
4: mi-peti yasur hennah — “whoever is simple, let him turn in here.” The address to the peti (simple, naive, H6612) is the same as in Proverbs 8:5; wisdom’s invitation is first directed to the most morally unformed. Chasar-lev — “lacking heart/mind” — the phrase from 7:7 recurs: the naïve young man who was vulnerable to the adulteress is now the target of wisdom’s specific invitation. The parallel between the two invitations (ch Proverbs 9:4 = ch Proverbs 9:16) is structural: both Wisdom and Folly use the same opening invitation-formula.
5: lechemu be-lachmiy — “come, eat of my bread.” The invitation to eat wisdom’s bread and drink her wine is the fullest expression of the feast metaphor in Proverbs. Sheitu be-yayin masakhti — “drink of the wine I have mixed.” The bread and wine of wisdom’s banquet are the material elements of the feast that lead to life; the later NT eucharistic resonances are not the text’s intention but arise from this shared symbolic vocabulary of the feast that transforms and sustains life.
6: 'izvu peta'im vi-chiyu — “leave your simple ways and live.” The imperative 'izvu (H5800, forsake, leave behind) applied to the peta'im (simple ways, the path of the naïve) frames wisdom’s invitation as requiring the abandonment of a prior way of life. Vi-chiyu — “and live” — the life-promise is the direct consequence of leaving simpleness behind. Ve-'ishru be-derekh binah — “and walk in the way of understanding”: 'ashar (H833, to go straight, to walk happily, to call blessed) is the same root as 'ashrei (blessed); to walk in understanding’s way is itself the blessed life.
7: yoser letz laqach lo qalon — “whoever corrects a mocker gets dishonor for himself.” Meyasser (H3256, to discipline, instruct, correct) directed at letz (H3887, the mocker/scoffer — the one who has made mockery a settled disposition) produces qalon (H7036, dishonor, shame) for the one who corrects. The wisdom tradition has a sober realism about the limits of instruction: it does not work on those who have chosen contempt as a lifestyle. Umochiah le-rasha' mumo — the one who reproves the wicked gets his mum (blemish/fault) thrown back at him.
8: 'al-tokach letz pen-yisne'eka — “do not rebuke a mocker or he will only hate you.” The prohibition is wisdom’s recognition that the receptivity of the addressee is a precondition of effective instruction. Hokach le-chakham ve-ye'ahavekha — “rebuke a wise person and he will love you.” The contrast is stark: the same act (rebuke) produces hatred in the mocker and love in the wise person. The wise person’s love-response to correction is the mark of genuine wisdom: he receives the rebuke as a gift rather than an insult.
9: ten le-chakham ve-yecham 'od — “give to a wise person and he will be wiser still.” The self-compounding nature of wisdom: chakham (wise) receiving instruction becomes yacham 'od (wiser still). Hoda' le-tzaddiq ve-yoseph leqach — “instruct a righteous person and he will increase in learning.” Tzaddiq (righteous, H6662) as a near-synonym of chakham (wise) in this parallel confirms the wisdom tradition’s equation of moral rectitude and cognitive wisdom: they are the same quality from different angles.
10: techillat chokmah yir'at YHWH — “the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom.” This is the thematic summary of Proverbs (cf. Proverbs 1:7: “the fear of the LORD is the beginning of knowledge”). Techillat (H8462, beginning, first principle — used of the beginning of a journey or the principal element of something) implies both temporal priority and logical foundationality: wisdom begins with and is grounded in yir'at YHWH. Ve-da'at qedoshim binah — “and knowledge of the Holy One is understanding.” Qedoshim (the Holy One/holy ones, H6918 plural) is rare as a divine title for YHWH alone; the plural may be a plural of majesty or an echo of the divine council.
11: bi yirbu yamecha — “through me your days will be multiplied.” Wisdom speaks in first person again, linking the long-life promise directly to wisdom’s presence rather than merely to wise behavior. Ve-yosifu lecha shenot chayyim — “and years of life will be added to you”: the promise is quantitative (more years) and qualitative (years of chayyim, true/full life). The life-promise is the consistent refrain of Proverbs’ invitation; here it is stated in the most direct form.
12: 'im-chakhamta chakhamta lakh — “if you are wise, you are wise for yourself.” The reflexive consequence of wisdom — it primarily benefits the one who acquires it — is a wisdom tradition motif (cf. Job 35:6-7; Proverbs 19:8). Ve-latzta levadecha tissa' — “and if you mock, you alone will bear it.” The isolation of the mocker is his own doing: he bears the consequences alone because he has rejected the community-forming function of wisdom. The binary of v12 (wise = benefit yourself; mock = bear it alone) expresses wisdom’s deeply personal stakes.
13: 'eshet kesilut homiyah — “Dame Folly is loud.” The personification of Folly as a woman ('eshet kesilot, ‘woman of foolishness/fools’ — a genitive of quality) creates the structural parallel to Wisdom-woman of vv1-6. Both are women, both call from elevated positions (vv3,14), both address the simple (vv4,16) with the same opening formula. Homiyah (H1993, clamorous, noisy — the same term used of the adulteress in Proverbs 7:11) links Dame Folly to the seductress. Peta'yut (H6612, simplemindedness) describes her character: she has the innocence-vocabulary but weaponizes it.
14: ve-yashevah le-fetach beitah — “she sits at the entrance to her house.” The contrast with Wisdom is spatial as well as moral: Wisdom’s servants go out actively to invite (v3); Folly sits (yashevah) passively at her own door. 'Al-kisse' meromei qaret — “on a seat at the heights of the city” — the same elevated location (meromei qaret) that Wisdom’s servants use (v3). The spatial parallel is an ironic mirror: Folly occupies wisdom’s calling-height but does so passively and deceptively.
15: liqro' le-'ovrei-derekh — “calling to those who pass by.” Ha-mesharim 'orchotehem — “those who are going straight on their way.” Yashir (H3474, straight, upright) used of the travelers’ paths indicates that Folly’s specific target is those who are already on a straight path — people not looking for alternative invitations. The seduction of those already walking the right path is more dangerous than the temptation of those already wandering.
16: mi-peti yasur hennah — “whoever is simple, let him turn in here.” The verbatim repetition of v4’s opening invitation (mi-peti yasur hennah and chasar-lev) is the structural climax of the two-invitation structure of Proverbs 9: both Wisdom and Folly use identical language to extend their invitations to the same audience. The difference is in what they offer: Wisdom offers bread and wine that lead to life (vv5-6); Folly offers stolen water and secret bread that lead to Sheol (vv17-18).
17: mayim genuvim yimtaqu — “stolen water is sweet.” Ganav (H1589, to steal) applied to water is the idiom of the forbidden secret; matok (H4966, sweet) captures the experiential appeal of transgression. Lechem setarim yin'am — “and food eaten in secret is delicious.” Sathar (H5641, hidden, secret — the verb for concealment) applied to food: the appeal of Folly is not that she offers better food than Wisdom but that the hiddenness and illicitness add a false sweetness. The wisdom tradition’s realism: transgression feels sweet in the moment.
18: ve-lo' yada' ki refa'im sham — “but he does not know that the Rephaim are there.” Refa'im (H7496, the shades/ghosts of the dead, those in Sheol) are the final revelation about Folly’s house: it is populated by the dead. Be-'imqei she'ol qeru'eyha — “that those she has invited are in the depths of Sheol.” The house of Folly is not a feast-hall but Sheol itself; her invitation is an invitation to death wearing the language of delight. This final verse closes the structural envelope of Proverbs 7-9: the adulteress’s house leads to Sheol (Proverbs 7:27), and Folly’s house is Sheol. Wisdom’s house leads to life (Proverbs 9:6, 11); folly’s house leads to death (Proverbs 9:18).
